Write a LinkedIn post for cloud about workflow. Angle: The cloud disruptions we’ve seen across 2025 and into February 2026 were warning shots⚠️ for every architecture leader in a regulated industry…not just technical glitches.

The cloud disruptions we’ve seen across 2025 and into February 2026 were warning shots⚠️ for every architecture leader in a regulated industry…not just technical glitches. When the backbone stutters, it doesn’t just stop music streaming…it stalls financial transactions & ops telemetry. We just witnessed a sustained gauntlet of infrastructure friction: 🔹2025: AWS DNS disruptions exposing shared naming dependencies. 🔹Feb 14: Google Cloud metadata issues impacting regional IAM & Vertex AI. 🔹Feb 20: Cloudflare’s 6-hour BYOIP withdrawal disrupting 1,100+ internet prefixes. 🔹Feb 27: Vertex AI & Gemini API service disruptions stalling Agentic workflows. 2026 is proving that centralization concentrates risk in shared control planes & global endpoints in ways we’re only now fully seeing, and it’s not the pinnacle of resilience it was claimed to be. The real shift is the legislative reaction, not the outage itself. 🏛️ The Rise of the “CREU” Bypass The DATA Act (Decentralized Access to Technology Alternatives) proposes a regulatory bypass for data centers that build their own off-grid power to leapfrog 4-6 year utility wait times. We're witnessing the formalization of a new infrastructure category: the Consumer Regulated Electric Utility (CREU). This is a structural carrot and stick realignment. 🥕The DATA Act (the Carrot) offers regulatory flexibility if a facility remains physically islanded. 🔨The GRID Act (the Stick) mandates that data centers at 20MW+ obtain power from sources other than the public grid to protect residential rate stability. 🔋 The Death of the Passive Load Data centers are becoming the grid. The shift is from incentives to accountability. If you want the compute in 2026, you have to architect the power delivery. The GPU cluster & the breaker box are now part of the same stack. 🛡️ Sovereignty as the New Resiliency Operational sovereignty cannot be outsourced in regulated sectors. Policy manuals do not help when the cloud goes dark. Hybrid Autonomy means systems that can run inference at the edge when the backbone is severed. If it cannot fail over locally…it is not resilient. It is lucky. 🔄 The Interoperability Wall Agentic AI requires a hardened data foundation. SAP’s 2026 Agentic Orchestration blueprint fails when it hits fragmented, siloed data. Master data consistency prevents agents from hallucinating or colliding. Autonomous agents become a liability if your adoption layer cannot handle real-time dependencies and disconnected operations. Success now depends on integration depth, not the model. The Bottom Line We are entering the era of the Sovereign Stack. The market will reward autonomous infrastructure, not just large compute clusters. If your AI strategy is 100% dependent on a public cloud DNS record, you don’t have a strategy. You have a prayer. 👇 Full LI breakout & architectural diagram below #AI #AIInfrastructure #EnterpriseArchitecture #DataSovereignty #AgenticAI #TechLeadership

Write a LinkedIn post for marketing about workflow. Angle: I replaced n8n with Claude Agent SDK for content engineering.

I replaced n8n with Claude Agent SDK for content engineering. Here's why agents beat workflows for content. Workflows follow rules. They don’t create, they checks boxes. If this → then that. Real content creation requires judgment, taste, and fresh context. "What topics should I write about?" depends on what customers want, new data on what’s getting results, and the company's marketing strategy. "What style should I use?" is equally hard. Launch announcements need a different voice than a data-driven post, or a personal story. Workflows require you to hardcode every decision and use pre-fixed system prompts. But writing isn't linear, it's creative. With an agent, when it generates a topic idea, it then runs through a chain of Skill files to pick the writing styles, and then draft a few variants. The agent adapts. When citing an expert, it writes in one style. When breaking a new story, it writes in another. A workflow that would need a 50+ node bowl of n8n spaghetti is now a 15-turn agent loop. There’s still a time and place for workflows, like for SEO/AIO systems. But the models are also ready for agent mode, and Claude Agent SDK gives you power of Claude Code, but custom trained for your content strategy, and a user friendly web app interface.

How AI adoption looks in companies today: Leadership: “Let’s use AI.” Sales: Uses AI for outreach. Marketing: Generates campaigns. Customer Support: Adds AI chatbots. HR: Screens candidates with AI. Finance: Uses AI for forecasting. Operations: Optimizes workflows with AI. Result: AI everywhere = ownership nowhere. What sets us apart from traditional consultants is simple: we’re operators first, advisors second. This gives us better insights into how employees are actually using AI tools in ways that might be risky, ways we may have never considered before. Here's what I'm seeing: in 2026, using AI across the company won't be optional anymore. Companies have finally gotten serious about doing something with AI, but if they don't have governance and use policies identified, they're rushing into a risky environment without any guardrails. The thing that breaks my heart is when I talk to business owners who say, "Oh yeah, we're using AI every day," thinking they're getting the most out of it. But when I inquire, it's always the same two things: they're only using it to write emails and summarize documents. They're misled, thinking they're an AI-forward company, but the reality is they're just using it like beginners. They don't know enough about the capabilities of these tools, and they're not thinking in AI-first terms. That's why the biggest mistake would be thinking you're already using AI as aggressively as you can when you're actually just scratching the surface.

Write a LinkedIn post for sales about workflow. Angle: What if you could replace an entire GTM team with a system of AI agents that coordinate themselves?

What if you could replace an entire GTM team with a system of AI agents that coordinate themselves? I just built it. Here's the problem: small businesses can't afford a full GTM team. You need a marketer, a sales ops person, a brand designer, and someone watching the infrastructure. Most founders are doing all of it themselves. So I built a system where AI agents do it instead. Not one chatbot trying to do everything — a team of specialized agents, each owning one domain: → Market Research / Writer — cold email, LinkedIn, A/B copy → GTM Ops — workflows, sending infrastructure, analytics pipelines → Designer / Brand — visual identity, landing pages, brand consistency → Agent Reliability Engineer — system health, incident response, security They're coordinated by a Chief of Staff agent that triages every request and delegates to the right specialist. No overlapping responsibilities. No gaps. The whole system runs on two feedback loops — one optimizing business outcomes, one improving the agents themselves. It gets better the longer it runs. What makes this different from stringing together a bunch of AI tools: • Each agent is configured through standardized identity files — modular, replaceable, scalable • Agents share a database but own their own tables — collaboration without chaos • New agents can be added in hours, not weeks • The system self-monitors through an Agent Reliability Engineer I built this with Claude Code, TypeScript, PostgreSQL, Discord, n8n, and Metabase. The reference architecture is open: → https://lnkd.in/guiBebhK If you're thinking about multi-agent systems for business operations — or you're a founder doing everything manually — I'd love to hear your take. #AI #MultiAgentSystems #GTM #StartupOps #AIEngineering

Write a LinkedIn post for devtools about workflow. Angle: Claude Code, Cursor, Kiro, GitHub Copilot, and Redis all independently built the same thing.

Claude Code, Cursor, Kiro, GitHub Copilot, and Redis all independently built the same thing. Nobody told them to. --- Last year everybody was hot on MCP. Every capability got wrapped in a protocol endpoint. This year the pendulum is swinging — developers are hitting context walls, accuracy is degrading, and the smartest builders are adding an orchestration layer above MCP rather than replacing it. The problem is straightforward: MCP gives your agent tools, but tools don't know about each other. They don't encode workflows. They don't carry domain knowledge. When I asked my AI agent to "add a new device to the IoT VLAN," it had 36 tools and zero guidance on the 6-step provisioning workflow that actually works. Every request was a cold-start reasoning problem. So I rebuilt my network skill as an intent-routing layer. A compact routing table maps natural language to specific workflows. Each workflow encodes tool order, parallelization, and verification steps. The result: "turn off the picture frame" goes from scanning 36 tools to exactly two MCP calls. No guessing. What's interesting is the convergence: 🔀 Claude Code → skills system with progressive disclosure (~30-50 tokens per skill at rest) 🔀 Kiro → Agent Skills with SKILL.md files + "Powers" (MCP servers wrapped in orchestration), pushing agentskills.io as an open standard 🔀 Cursor → modular .mdc files, hard 40-tool limit 🔀 GitHub Copilot → path-specific .instructions.md with scoped context 🔀 Redis → vector search to find 3-5 relevant tools instead of loading 167 Six different teams. Same conclusion: don't dump everything into context. Route to what's relevant. Load on demand. Kiro is especially telling — they explicitly separate Skills (on-demand capabilities), Steering (always-on directives), and Powers (MCP + rules). That taxonomy mirrors what every builder is discovering organically. The data supports it. Redis benchmarks: tool accuracy jumped from 42% to 85% when filtered. Anthropic's Tool Search improved accuracy from 49% to 74%. Chroma found 20-50% accuracy drops as context scaled. MCP won the standards war. The second-order battle is orchestration — and skills are how you win it. Full writeup with data, architecture, and a concrete walkthrough: https://lnkd.in/gmxcHqUJ --- #MCP #AIAgents #AgentExperience #DevTools #ContextEngineering #AI #Architecture

In the past weeks we experienced a steep innovation curve in Personal AI Assistants. Stay tuned. On Friday March 27th we publish DefenseClaw on GitHub https://lnkd.in/ehuy4AQ7. Transformative shifts in the industry with Clawdbot-> Moltbot ->Openclaw, including the latest the addition of security and privacy provided by NemoClaw that uses Openshell to enforce policy-based privacy and security guardrails. Users gain control over how agents behave and handle data. DefenseClaw is the agentic governance layer that sits on top of OpenShell and includes Cisco’s open sourced scanners that developers can deploy in under five minutes. 1) DefenseClaw scans every skill, every tool, every plugin, before it’s allowed into your claw environment and every piece of code generated by the claw gets scanned. The scan engine includes five tools: - skill-scanner, - mcp-scanner, - a2a-scanner, - CodeGuard static analysis - AI bill-of-materials generator. 2) The DefenseClaw content scanner inspects every message flowing in and out of the agent at the execution loop itself. Claws are self-evolving systems. A skill that was clean on Tuesday can start exfiltrating data on Thursday. 3) It enforces block and allow lists. When you block a skill, its sandbox permissions are revoked, its files are quarantined, and the agent gets an error if it tries to invoke it. When you block an MCP server, the endpoint is removed from the sandbox network allow-list and OpenShell denies all connections. This happens in under two seconds, no restart required.
